title: Operators & Expressions
description: Operators are the verbs of Java — they define what happens to your data. This chapter gives you a complete map of all operator categories, their precedence rules, and the tricky edge cases in arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise, and ternary operations that appear constantly in CSE110 output-prediction questions.

sections.content: 🗺️ Operator Categories — The Big Picture

Java has **7 categories** of operators. Understanding which category an operator belongs to tells you its precedence (priority) and behavior. The most common exam traps involve **mixed categories** in a single expression.

sections.label: Precedence Memory Aid
sections.content: High to Low: **Unary → Multiplicative (`* / %`) → Additive (`+ -`) → Relational → Equality → Bitwise → Logical AND → Logical OR → Ternary → Assignment**. When in doubt, **use parentheses**.
➕ Arithmetic Operators — Tricky Behaviors

The basic arithmetic operators hide several non-obvious behaviors that Java uses to catch students off guard in exams. You already know `+` string concatenation from Chapter 1 — here we focus on the **numeric edge cases**.

sections.title: Arithmetic Edge Cases

sections.label: Floating-Point Precision
sections.content: `0.1 + 0.2` does NOT equal `0.3` in Java (or any language using IEEE 754). The result is `0.30000000000000004`. **Never use `==` to compare doubles**. Use `Math.abs(a - b) < 1e-9` instead.
⚖️ Relational & Equality Operators

Relational operators return `boolean`. The trickiest part is `==` — it means **reference equality** for objects but **value equality** for primitives. For Strings and all objects, use `.equals()` for value comparison.

sections.title: == vs .equals() — The Critical Distinction

sections.content: 🧠 Logical Operators — Short-Circuit Evaluation

Java has **two types** of logical operators: **short-circuit** (`&&`, `||`) and **non-short-circuit** (`&`, `|`). The difference determines whether the second operand is even evaluated — this has major implications for performance and **side effect behavior** in expressions.

rows: If LEFT is `false`, RIGHT is **never evaluated**
If LEFT is `true`,  RIGHT is **never evaluated**
Both sides **always** evaluated. Also works on integers
Both sides **always** evaluated. Also works on integers

sections.title: Short-Circuit — When Side Effects Matter

sections.label: Null-Safe Pattern
sections.content: `if (obj != null && obj.method())` — the `&&` short-circuit ensures `obj.method()` is only called if `obj != null`. If you used `&` instead, it would always call `obj.method()`, throwing `NullPointerException` when `obj` is null. This pattern is everywhere in production Java.
🔢 Bitwise Operators on Integers
Bitwise operators work on **individual bits** of integer values. They are fast, low-level, and appear in CSE110 questions testing your ability to predict output from bit manipulation.

sections.title: Bitwise Operations — With Binary Breakdown

sections.label: ~n = -(n+1)
sections.content: The bitwise NOT `~n` is equivalent to `-(n+1)` in Java's two's complement representation. So `~12 = -13`, `~0 = -1`, `~(-1) = 0`. This is a useful identity to know for output prediction questions.
❓ The Ternary Operator

The ternary operator `condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse` is a **single expression** that evaluates to one of two values. It is the only **three-operand** operator in Java. Key rule: both branches must be **type-compatible**.

sections.title: Ternary — Common Patterns & Traps

sections.label: Ternary Precedence Trap
sections.content: `"Label: " + x > 0 ? "yes" : "no"` is parsed as `("Label: " + x) > 0 ? ...` because `+` has higher precedence than `?:`. This usually causes a compile error (comparing String to 0). Always **wrap ternaries in parentheses** when concatenating: `"Label: " + (x > 0 ? "yes" : "no")`.
📝 Assignment & Compound Assignment
Compound assignment operators (`+=`, `-=`, etc.) are shorthand and include an **implicit narrow cast**. This makes them behave differently from their expanded form in some cases.

sections.title: Compound Assignment — The Implicit Cast

sections.content: 🧪 Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Operator Precedence Minefield
Exercise 2: Short-Circuit with Side Effects
sections.explanation: A-B: `dec()` runs (x=-1, returns false). `&&` short-circuits: `inc()` NEVER runs. x stays -1. C-D: `inc()` runs (x=1, returns true). `||` short-circuits: `dec()` NEVER runs. x stays 1. E-F: Eager `&` evaluates BOTH sides always. `dec()` runs (x=-1), then `inc()` runs (x=0). Final x=0.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Bitwise Operations Output
Exercise 4: Ternary Nesting & Type Promotion
sections.explanation: x=50: not >90, not >75, not >60, but >40 → **D**. Ternary type promotion: `true ? 42 : 42.5` — both branches must be compatible. `42` (int) promotes to `42.0` (double) to match `42.5`. So even when true branch is taken, result is double **42.0**. `false ? 42 : 42.5` → **42.5** (double). `min` of 5 and 10 is **5**.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Three Expression Errors
sections.error: Three bugs: Line 5: **Runtime Error** — using eager `&` means `a/b` is evaluated even when `b==0`, causing `ArithmeticException`. Should use `&&` (short-circuit). Line 11: **Compile Error** — `x = true && y = false` is parsed as `x = (true && y) = false` which is invalid. Should be `x == true && y == false` (use `==` for comparison). Line 17: **Logical Error** — `d1 == 0.3` is false due to floating-point imprecision (`0.1+0.2 = 0.30000000000000004`). Should use `Math.abs(d1 - 0.3) < 1e-9`.
sections.explanation: These three bugs represent three different error types: a runtime crash from eager evaluation (Bug 1), a compile error from confusing assignment and comparison operators (Bug 2), and a silent logical error from floating-point imprecision (Bug 3). All three are real-world Java pitfalls.

sections.title: Exercise 6: The Increment Expression Puzzle
sections.explanation: Trace `b = a++ + ++a + a++` step by step (left to right): `a++` uses 5, a→6. `++a` increments first to 7, uses 7. `a++` uses 7, a→8. So b = 5+7+7 = **18**, final a = **8**. Then: `x++=3` is `x++ == 3` → postfix uses current value 3, compares == 3 → **true**, x becomes 4. `++x == 5` → prefix increments x to 5 first, 5==5 → **true**, x is **5**.

sections.content: 📋 Operator Quick Reference

rows: Same result, but `&` evaluates both sides; `&&` short-circuits
Compiles OK (`+=` has implicit cast). Result: `(byte)210 = -46`
